  • Hide All Code
  • Download Rmd

R Notebook

Materials used
S09_multiple.pdf
https://brianreedpowers.github.io/stat340/prediction2.html

Multi Linear Regression

data(mtcars)

mtc_model <- lm(qsec ~ 1 + disp + hp + wt, data = mtcars)
summary(mtc_model)

Call:
lm(formula = qsec ~ 1 + disp + hp + wt, data = mtcars)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-1.8121 -0.3125 -0.0245  0.3544  3.3693 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 17.965050   0.849663  21.144  < 2e-16 ***
disp        -0.006622   0.004166  -1.590  0.12317    
hp          -0.022953   0.004603  -4.986 2.88e-05 ***
wt           1.485283   0.429172   3.461  0.00175 ** 
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 1.062 on 28 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6808,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.6466 
F-statistic: 19.91 on 3 and 28 DF,  p-value: 4.134e-07
Estimate: Fitted Coefficients
PT(>|t|): p-value for testing wheather each coffeceient is 0
Residual Standard error: model's typical prediction error size
Multiple R Squared: Proportion of variation explained
F-Statistic: Whether the model as a whole explains anything beyond the intercept only model

For wt, holding disp and hp fixed. A 1 unit increase in wt is associated with an average increase of about 1.49 in qsec

T- value -> estimate / std.error for disp is -1.59. A bigger t-value stronger evidence that the coefficient is not 0.

RSE: The models prediction errors are typically 1.062 seconds
Degrees of Freedom: n is 32, minus 3 predictors + 1 intercept is 28
Multiple R-Squared: About 68% of the variability in qsec is explained by the model using dsip, hp, and wt
F-Statistic: F is 19.91 with a p-value of 4.134 so we reject the null and conclude the model as a whole has signficant explanatory power. And using the variables is much better then using no predictors at all.

We emphasize that the model is usefull overall. But that disp is not useful in the model once hp and wt are already in the model.

qsec = 17.965 - 0.00662(disp) - 0.02295(hp) + 1.485(wt)

2. Checking assumptions with residuals

par(mfrow = c(2,2))
plot(mtc_model)
par(mfrow = c(1,1))
Residuals vs Fitted (top left)
We are checking linearity and mean of residuals roughly equals 0 with no systemic pattern

The points are mostly scattered aroound 0, the red line has a slight downward pattern with slight curvature
There is no strong violation but suggests possible mild nonlinearity
Acceptable for the class

Q-Q Plot (top right)
Checks if residuals are normally distributed

Middle points lie close to the line
The tails deviate which suggest slight non-normality, especially in the extremes
Normalality assumption isn't perfect but still acceptable for most cases
But be careful with p-values if deviations are too large

Scale Location (bottom left)
Homoscadestacity

Curved uowards, spread increases a bit for higher fitted values

Mild heteroscadasticity, variance slightly increases as fitted values increase
But not severe, still acceptable

Residuals vs Leverage (bottom right)
Influenctial Points

Most points clustered low-average -> normal
A few labeled points (Maserati Bora) -> (~0.5) abd (Merc 230) -> Large residuals
Cook's distance lines shown (dashed)

3. Categorical predictors with two levels

data(mtcars)

mtcars$am <- factor(mtcars$am) # automatic = 0, manual = 1
mtcars$vs <- factor(mtcars$vs) # v-shaped = 0, straight = 1

cat_model <- lm(qsec ~ 1 + am + vs, data = mtcars)
summary(cat_model)

Call:
lm(formula = qsec ~ 1 + am + vs, data = mtcars)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-1.77902 -0.37789  0.01687  0.65157  2.91187 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  17.1303     0.2754  62.195  < 2e-16 ***
am1          -1.3091     0.3791  -3.453  0.00172 ** 
vs1           2.8579     0.3753   7.614 2.15e-08 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 1.038 on 29 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6842,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.6624 
F-statistic: 31.41 on 2 and 29 DF,  p-value: 5.519e-08

4. Interactions between two catagorical predictors

## am:vs = interaction only 
## am * vs = main effect plus interactions
library(interactions)


interact_model <- lm(qsec ~ 1 + am * vs, data = mtcars)
summary(interact_model)

cat_plot(interact_model,
         pred = "vs",
         modx = "am",
         geom = "line",
         plot.points = TRUE,
         data = mtcars,
         interval = FALSE)
Looking at am1:vs1, we can look at the combined effect of being in both categorical values together. 
am1 = manual, and vs1 = staight engine. For cars that are both, the predicted qseec is .07 higher then what you would expect from just adding the seperate main effects of am and vs alone. But it is not significant, p-value .91% so a simpler model qsec ~ am + vs is sufficient

5. Categorical predictors with more than two levels

data(iris)

iris$Species <- factor(iris$Species, levels = c("setosa", "versicolor", "virginica"))

sepal.fit <- lm(Sepal.Length ~ 1 + Species, data = iris)
summary(sepal.fit)

Call:
lm(formula = Sepal.Length ~ 1 + Species, data = iris)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-1.6880 -0.3285 -0.0060  0.3120  1.3120 

Coefficients:
                  Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)         5.0060     0.0728  68.762  < 2e-16 ***
Speciesversicolor   0.9300     0.1030   9.033 8.77e-16 ***
Speciesvirginica    1.5820     0.1030  15.366  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 0.5148 on 147 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6187,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.6135 
F-statistic: 119.3 on 2 and 147 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
aggregate(Sepal.Length ~ Species, data = iris, FUN = mean)

model.matrix(sepal.fit)[1:10, ]
   (Intercept) Speciesversicolor Speciesvirginica
1            1                 0                0
2            1                 0                0
3            1                 0                0
4            1                 0                0
5            1                 0                0
6            1                 0                0
7            1                 0                0
8            1                 0                0
9            1                 0                0
10           1                 0                0
Setosa is the baseline so the intercept is the mean sepal length for setosa

Speciesversicolor = difference between versicolor and setosa
Speciesviginica = difference between virginica and setosa

So 
Setosa: 5.006
Versicolor: 5.006 + 0.96 = 5.936
Virginica: 5.006 + 1.582 = 6.588

6. Interaction example where interaction matters

set.seed(340)

Food <- factor(rep(c("Hot Dog", "Ice Cream"), each = 40))
Condiment <- factor(rep(rep(c("Chocolate", "Mustard"), each = 20), 2))

# Build a strong interaction:
# Hot Dog + Mustard high
# Ice Cream + Chocolate high
Enjoyment <- c(
  rnorm(20, mean = 65, sd = 4),   # Hot Dog + Chocolate
  rnorm(20, mean = 90, sd = 4),   # Hot Dog + Mustard
  rnorm(20, mean = 92, sd = 4),   # Ice Cream + Chocolate
  rnorm(20, mean = 62, sd = 4)    # Ice Cream + Mustard
)

frost_like <- data.frame(Food, Condiment, Enjoyment)

# No interaction
m1 <- lm(Enjoyment ~ 1 + Food + Condiment, data = frost_like)
summary(m1)

Call:
lm(formula = Enjoyment ~ 1 + Food + Condiment, data = frost_like)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-22.1029 -13.9286   0.0561  14.3296  22.0359 

Coefficients:
                 Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)       78.1417     2.8778  27.153   <2e-16 ***
FoodIce Cream     -0.5970     3.3230  -0.180    0.858    
CondimentMustard  -0.8354     3.3230  -0.251    0.802    
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 14.86 on 77 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.001238,  Adjusted R-squared:  -0.0247 
F-statistic: 0.04774 on 2 and 77 DF,  p-value: 0.9534
# With interaction
m2 <- lm(Enjoyment ~ 1 + Food * Condiment, data = frost_like)
summary(m2)

Call:
lm(formula = Enjoyment ~ 1 + Food * Condiment, data = frost_like)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-8.0152 -2.4182  0.1784  2.5018  8.0965 

Coefficients:
                               Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)                     64.0540     0.8615   74.36   <2e-16 ***
FoodIce Cream                   27.5785     1.2183   22.64   <2e-16 ***
CondimentMustard                27.3401     1.2183   22.44   <2e-16 ***
FoodIce Cream:CondimentMustard -56.3510     1.7229  -32.71   <2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 3.853 on 76 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.9337,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.9311 
F-statistic:   357 on 3 and 76 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
What we see in the interaction between Ice Cream and Mustard
In m1, we see that it is misleading because we are averaging between two opposite patterns
In m2, we see that the interaction terms are significant because the condiment effect flips depending on the food

In simple terms we can see that becayse the best condiment depends on the food. Without interaction both main effects can look non-significant; once the interaction is added, everything becomes signficant.

7. Nonlinear relationships: adding a squared term

plot(mtcars$hp, mtcars$mpg,
     main = "mpg vs hp",
     xlab = "hp", ylab = "mpg")

# Linear model
mtc_lm <- lm(mpg ~ 1 + hp, data = mtcars)
summary(mtc_lm)

Call:
lm(formula = mpg ~ 1 + hp, data = mtcars)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-5.7121 -2.1122 -0.8854  1.5819  8.2360 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 30.09886    1.63392  18.421  < 2e-16 ***
hp          -0.06823    0.01012  -6.742 1.79e-07 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 3.863 on 30 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6024,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.5892 
F-statistic: 45.46 on 1 and 30 DF,  p-value: 1.788e-07
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))

plot(fitted(mtc_lm), resid(mtc_lm),
     main = "Residuals vs Fitted: linear",
     xlab = "Fitted values", ylab = "Residuals")
abline(h = 0, lty = 2)

# Quadratic model
mtc_lm2 <- lm(mpg ~ 1 + hp + I(hp^2), data = mtcars)
summary(mtc_lm2)

Call:
lm(formula = mpg ~ 1 + hp + I(hp^2), data = mtcars)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-4.5512 -1.6027 -0.6977  1.5509  8.7213 

Coefficients:
              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  4.041e+01  2.741e+00  14.744 5.23e-15 ***
hp          -2.133e-01  3.488e-02  -6.115 1.16e-06 ***
I(hp^2)      4.208e-04  9.844e-05   4.275 0.000189 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 3.077 on 29 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.7561,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7393 
F-statistic: 44.95 on 2 and 29 DF,  p-value: 1.301e-09
plot(fitted(mtc_lm2), resid(mtc_lm2),
     main = "Residuals vs Fitted: quadratic",
     xlab = "Fitted values", ylab = "Residuals")
abline(h = 0, lty = 2)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

plot(mtcars$hp, mtcars$mpg,
     xlab = "hp", ylab = "mpg",
     main = "Linear vs quadratic fit")

hp_grid <- seq(min(mtcars$hp), max(mtcars$hp), length.out = 200)

lines(hp_grid,
      predict(mtc_lm, newdata = data.frame(hp = hp_grid)),
      lwd = 2)

lines(hp_grid,
      predict(mtc_lm2, newdata = data.frame(hp = hp_grid)),
      lwd = 2, lty = 2)

Looking at MPG we can see that the relationship is curved. MPG drops quickly at first then levels off. The fix is to adda quadratic term using I(hp^2)

8. Practice: better predictor in simple regression

data(trees)

ht_lm <- lm(Volume ~ 1 + Height, data = trees)
gr_lm <- lm(Volume ~ 1 + Girth, data = trees)

summary(ht_lm)

Call:
lm(formula = Volume ~ 1 + Height, data = trees)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-21.274  -9.894  -2.894  12.068  29.852 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) -87.1236    29.2731  -2.976 0.005835 ** 
Height        1.5433     0.3839   4.021 0.000378 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 13.4 on 29 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.3579,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.3358 
F-statistic: 16.16 on 1 and 29 DF,  p-value: 0.0003784
summary(gr_lm)

Call:
lm(formula = Volume ~ 1 + Girth, data = trees)

Residuals:
   Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
-8.065 -3.107  0.152  3.495  9.587 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) -36.9435     3.3651  -10.98 7.62e-12 ***
Girth         5.0659     0.2474   20.48  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 4.252 on 29 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.9353,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.9331 
F-statistic: 419.4 on 1 and 29 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))

plot(trees$Height, trees$Volume,
     main = "Volume vs Height",
     xlab = "Height", ylab = "Volume")
abline(ht_lm, lwd = 2)

plot(trees$Girth, trees$Volume,
     main = "Volume vs Girth",
     xlab = "Girth", ylab = "Volume")
abline(gr_lm, lwd = 2)

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

9. Practice non-linear: geometry intuition

gr_sq_lm <- lm(Volume ~ 1 + Girth + I(Girth^2), data = trees)

summary(gr_lm)

Call:
lm(formula = Volume ~ 1 + Girth, data = trees)

Residuals:
   Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
-8.065 -3.107  0.152  3.495  9.587 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) -36.9435     3.3651  -10.98 7.62e-12 ***
Girth         5.0659     0.2474   20.48  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 4.252 on 29 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.9353,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.9331 
F-statistic: 419.4 on 1 and 29 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
summary(gr_sq_lm)

Call:
lm(formula = Volume ~ 1 + Girth + I(Girth^2), data = trees)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-5.4889 -2.4293 -0.3718  2.0764  7.6447 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 10.78627   11.22282   0.961 0.344728    
Girth       -2.09214    1.64734  -1.270 0.214534    
I(Girth^2)   0.25454    0.05817   4.376 0.000152 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 3.335 on 28 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.9616,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.9588 
F-statistic: 350.5 on 2 and 28 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
anova(gr_lm, gr_sq_lm)
Analysis of Variance Table

Model 1: Volume ~ 1 + Girth
Model 2: Volume ~ 1 + Girth + I(Girth^2)
  Res.Df    RSS Df Sum of Sq      F    Pr(>F)    
1     29 524.30                                  
2     28 311.38  1    212.92 19.146 0.0001524 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

10. Clean workflow for and Stat 340 problem

### STAT 340 Multiple Regression Walkthrough

### 1) Main multiple regression example
data(mtcars)
mtc_model <- lm(qsec ~ 1 + disp + hp + wt, data = mtcars)
summary(mtc_model)

Call:
lm(formula = qsec ~ 1 + disp + hp + wt, data = mtcars)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-1.8121 -0.3125 -0.0245  0.3544  3.3693 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 17.965050   0.849663  21.144  < 2e-16 ***
disp        -0.006622   0.004166  -1.590  0.12317    
hp          -0.022953   0.004603  -4.986 2.88e-05 ***
wt           1.485283   0.429172   3.461  0.00175 ** 
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 1.062 on 28 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6808,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.6466 
F-statistic: 19.91 on 3 and 28 DF,  p-value: 4.134e-07
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(mtc_model)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))


rss <- sum(resid(mtc_model)^2)
n <- nrow(mtcars)
p <- 3
rse <- sqrt(rss / (n - (p + 1)))
rss
[1] 31.59412
rse
[1] 1.062244
summary(mtc_model)$r.squared
[1] 0.6808292
summary(mtc_model)$adj.r.squared
[1] 0.6466324
summary(mtc_model)$fstatistic
   value    numdf    dendf 
19.90911  3.00000 28.00000 
### 2) R^2 and correlation
mt.fit <- lm(hp ~ 1 + wt, data = mtcars)
summary(mt.fit)$r.squared
[1] 0.4339488
cor(mtcars$wt, mtcars$hp)^2
[1] 0.4339488
mt.fit2 <- lm(qsec ~ 1 + disp + wt + hp, data = mtcars)
summary(mt.fit2)$r.squared
[1] 0.6808292
cor(mtcars$qsec, predict(mt.fit2))^2
[1] 0.6808292
### 3) Categorical predictors and interaction
mtcars$am <- factor(mtcars$am)
mtcars$vs <- factor(mtcars$vs)

cat_model <- lm(qsec ~ 1 + am + vs, data = mtcars)
summary(cat_model)

Call:
lm(formula = qsec ~ 1 + am + vs, data = mtcars)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-1.77902 -0.37789  0.01687  0.65157  2.91187 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  17.1303     0.2754  62.195  < 2e-16 ***
am1          -1.3091     0.3791  -3.453  0.00172 ** 
vs1           2.8579     0.3753   7.614 2.15e-08 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 1.038 on 29 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6842,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.6624 
F-statistic: 31.41 on 2 and 29 DF,  p-value: 5.519e-08
interact_model <- lm(qsec ~ 1 + am * vs, data = mtcars)
summary(interact_model)

Call:
lm(formula = qsec ~ 1 + am * vs, data = mtcars)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-1.80000 -0.35429  0.03786  0.66687  2.93286 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 17.14250    0.30497  56.210  < 2e-16 ***
am1         -1.34583    0.52823  -2.548   0.0166 *  
vs1          2.82464    0.50245   5.622 5.09e-06 ***
am1:vs1      0.07869    0.77325   0.102   0.9197    
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 1.056 on 28 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6843,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.6505 
F-statistic: 20.23 on 3 and 28 DF,  p-value: 3.556e-07
# Optional:
# library(interactions)
# cat_plot(interact_model, pred = "vs", modx = "am",
#          geom = "line", plot.points = TRUE,
#          data = mtcars, interval = FALSE)

### 4) Multiple categorical levels
data(iris)
iris$Species <- factor(iris$Species,
                       levels = c("setosa", "versicolor", "virginica"))

sepal.fit <- lm(Sepal.Length ~ 1 + Species, data = iris)
summary(sepal.fit)

Call:
lm(formula = Sepal.Length ~ 1 + Species, data = iris)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-1.6880 -0.3285 -0.0060  0.3120  1.3120 

Coefficients:
                  Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)         5.0060     0.0728  68.762  < 2e-16 ***
Speciesversicolor   0.9300     0.1030   9.033 8.77e-16 ***
Speciesvirginica    1.5820     0.1030  15.366  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 0.5148 on 147 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6187,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.6135 
F-statistic: 119.3 on 2 and 147 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
aggregate(Sepal.Length ~ Species, data = iris, FUN = mean)
model.matrix(sepal.fit)[1:10, ]
   (Intercept) Speciesversicolor Speciesvirginica
1            1                 0                0
2            1                 0                0
3            1                 0                0
4            1                 0                0
5            1                 0                0
6            1                 0                0
7            1                 0                0
8            1                 0                0
9            1                 0                0
10           1                 0                0
### 5) Interaction example with simulated food/condiment data
set.seed(340)
Food <- factor(rep(c("Hot Dog", "Ice Cream"), each = 40))
Condiment <- factor(rep(rep(c("Chocolate", "Mustard"), each = 20), 2))
Enjoyment <- c(
  rnorm(20, mean = 65, sd = 4),
  rnorm(20, mean = 90, sd = 4),
  rnorm(20, mean = 92, sd = 4),
  rnorm(20, mean = 62, sd = 4)
)
frost_like <- data.frame(Food, Condiment, Enjoyment)

m1 <- lm(Enjoyment ~ 1 + Food + Condiment, data = frost_like)
m2 <- lm(Enjoyment ~ 1 + Food * Condiment, data = frost_like)
summary(m1)

Call:
lm(formula = Enjoyment ~ 1 + Food + Condiment, data = frost_like)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-22.1029 -13.9286   0.0561  14.3296  22.0359 

Coefficients:
                 Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)       78.1417     2.8778  27.153   <2e-16 ***
FoodIce Cream     -0.5970     3.3230  -0.180    0.858    
CondimentMustard  -0.8354     3.3230  -0.251    0.802    
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 14.86 on 77 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.001238,  Adjusted R-squared:  -0.0247 
F-statistic: 0.04774 on 2 and 77 DF,  p-value: 0.9534
summary(m2)

Call:
lm(formula = Enjoyment ~ 1 + Food * Condiment, data = frost_like)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-8.0152 -2.4182  0.1784  2.5018  8.0965 

Coefficients:
                               Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)                     64.0540     0.8615   74.36   <2e-16 ***
FoodIce Cream                   27.5785     1.2183   22.64   <2e-16 ***
CondimentMustard                27.3401     1.2183   22.44   <2e-16 ***
FoodIce Cream:CondimentMustard -56.3510     1.7229  -32.71   <2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 3.853 on 76 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.9337,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.9311 
F-statistic:   357 on 3 and 76 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
### 6) Nonlinear relationship
plot(mtcars$hp, mtcars$mpg, xlab = "hp", ylab = "mpg")

mtc_lm <- lm(mpg ~ 1 + hp, data = mtcars)
mtc_lm2 <- lm(mpg ~ 1 + hp + I(hp^2), data = mtcars)

summary(mtc_lm)

Call:
lm(formula = mpg ~ 1 + hp, data = mtcars)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-5.7121 -2.1122 -0.8854  1.5819  8.2360 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 30.09886    1.63392  18.421  < 2e-16 ***
hp          -0.06823    0.01012  -6.742 1.79e-07 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 3.863 on 30 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6024,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.5892 
F-statistic: 45.46 on 1 and 30 DF,  p-value: 1.788e-07
summary(mtc_lm2)

Call:
lm(formula = mpg ~ 1 + hp + I(hp^2), data = mtcars)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-4.5512 -1.6027 -0.6977  1.5509  8.7213 

Coefficients:
              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  4.041e+01  2.741e+00  14.744 5.23e-15 ***
hp          -2.133e-01  3.488e-02  -6.115 1.16e-06 ***
I(hp^2)      4.208e-04  9.844e-05   4.275 0.000189 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 3.077 on 29 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.7561,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7393 
F-statistic: 44.95 on 2 and 29 DF,  p-value: 1.301e-09
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))

plot(fitted(mtc_lm), resid(mtc_lm),
     main = "Linear residuals", xlab = "Fitted", ylab = "Residuals")
abline(h = 0, lty = 2)

plot(fitted(mtc_lm2), resid(mtc_lm2),
     main = "Quadratic residuals", xlab = "Fitted", ylab = "Residuals")
abline(h = 0, lty = 2)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))


plot(mtcars$hp, mtcars$mpg, xlab = "hp", ylab = "mpg")
hp_grid <- seq(min(mtcars$hp), max(mtcars$hp), length.out = 200)
lines(hp_grid, predict(mtc_lm, newdata = data.frame(hp = hp_grid)), lwd = 2)
lines(hp_grid, predict(mtc_lm2, newdata = data.frame(hp = hp_grid)), lwd = 2, lty = 2)

### 7) Practice page: trees example
data(trees)
ht_lm <- lm(Volume ~ 1 + Height, data = trees)
gr_lm <- lm(Volume ~ 1 + Girth, data = trees)
summary(ht_lm)

Call:
lm(formula = Volume ~ 1 + Height, data = trees)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-21.274  -9.894  -2.894  12.068  29.852 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) -87.1236    29.2731  -2.976 0.005835 ** 
Height        1.5433     0.3839   4.021 0.000378 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 13.4 on 29 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.3579,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.3358 
F-statistic: 16.16 on 1 and 29 DF,  p-value: 0.0003784
summary(gr_lm)

Call:
lm(formula = Volume ~ 1 + Girth, data = trees)

Residuals:
   Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
-8.065 -3.107  0.152  3.495  9.587 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) -36.9435     3.3651  -10.98 7.62e-12 ***
Girth         5.0659     0.2474   20.48  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 4.252 on 29 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.9353,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.9331 
F-statistic: 419.4 on 1 and 29 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))

plot(trees$Height, trees$Volume, main = "Volume vs Height",
     xlab = "Height", ylab = "Volume")
abline(ht_lm, lwd = 2)

plot(trees$Girth, trees$Volume, main = "Volume vs Girth",
     xlab = "Girth", ylab = "Volume")
abline(gr_lm, lwd = 2)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))


gr_sq_lm <- lm(Volume ~ 1 + Girth + I(Girth^2), data = trees)
summary(gr_sq_lm)

Call:
lm(formula = Volume ~ 1 + Girth + I(Girth^2), data = trees)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-5.4889 -2.4293 -0.3718  2.0764  7.6447 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 10.78627   11.22282   0.961 0.344728    
Girth       -2.09214    1.64734  -1.270 0.214534    
I(Girth^2)   0.25454    0.05817   4.376 0.000152 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 3.335 on 28 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.9616,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.9588 
F-statistic: 350.5 on 2 and 28 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
anova(gr_lm, gr_sq_lm)
Analysis of Variance Table

Model 1: Volume ~ 1 + Girth
Model 2: Volume ~ 1 + Girth + I(Girth^2)
  Res.Df    RSS Df Sum of Sq      F    Pr(>F)    
1     29 524.30                                  
2     28 311.38  1    212.92 19.146 0.0001524 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

To do

1. Categorical predictors with more than two levels
2. Interaction examples where interaction matters
3. Non-linear relationships: adding a squared term
4. Practice-Page comparison: better predictor in simple regression
5. Pratice-page nonlinear idea: geometry intuition
6. A clean workflow for any STAT340 regression problem
7. Common exam traps
8. Full Script
9. What each example is teaching us
